name: Cow milk, pasteurized, vitamin D added, 3.25% fat (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh whole milk (~97 mL; density ~1.03 g/mL)

components.name: lactose
components.source: USDA FDC 746782 (Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D; SR Legacy)
components.evidence: DOMINANT carbon/energy source of milk; disaccharide (glucose+galactose), MW 342.3. mM computed as 48 g/L / 342.3.

components.name: D-glucose
components.source: Dairy chemistry (Fox & McSweeney, Dairy Chemistry & Biochemistry); free monosaccharide fraction
components.evidence: Free glucose in bovine milk is only trace (~5-15 mg/100g); most glucose is bound in lactose. Real but minor.

components.name: D-galactose
components.source: Dairy chemistry literature; free monosaccharide fraction
components.evidence: Trace free galactose (~5-20 mg/100g); USDA lists free galactose as 0. Real but minor; bulk galactose is bound in lactose.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Bovine milk citrate 8-11 mmol/L (Fox & McSweeney Dairy Chemistry; Garnsworthy 2006 PMID 12237966 citrate vs ketones); FooDB FOOD00892
components.evidence: KEY OMISSION added: citrate is the principal organic acid of milk (~1.5-2.1 g/L as citric acid). mM = 1.7 g/L / 192.12. Fermentable by lactic acid bacteria (citrate->diacetyl).

components.name: orotic acid
components.source: Larson & Hegarty / PMID 1958838 (mean 0.618 mmol/L, range 0.33-1.04); Agrarforschung Schweiz 2008 (6.3-7.6 mg/100g)
components.evidence: Pyrimidine-pathway organic acid unique to (and abundant in) milk; 60-100 mg/L. MW 156.1. Added as missing.

components.name: L-lactate
components.source: FooDB FOOD00892; dairy chemistry (fresh pasteurized milk)
components.evidence: Low in FRESH pasteurized milk (~0.003-0.02 g/100g); rises only with microbial fermentation/souring. Kept at trace with note.

components.name: L-malate
components.evidence: Minor TCA-cycle organic acid present in milk at trace levels.

components.name: total protein
components.evidence: ~80% casein, ~20% whey; amino acids below are the protein-derived profile (not free).

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Glutamate+glutamine; most abundant AA in casein.

components.name: L-glutamine
components.source: FooDB FOOD00892 (Levoglutamide); USDA reports as glutamate+glutamine pool
components.evidence: Present; not separately quantified from glutamate in USDA. 'Levoglutamide' in uncovered = L-glutamine.

components.name: L-valine
components.evidence: Essential BCAA (was MISSING from current list).

components.name: L-serine
components.evidence: Current list had 'D-Serine' (mislabeled); milk protein serine is L.

components.name: L-phenylalanine
components.evidence: Essential AA (was MISSING from current list).

components.name: total fat
components.evidence: Milk fat, ~66% saturated. Individual fatty acids below.

components.name: butyric acid (4:0)
components.evidence: Short-chain FA characteristic of milk (was MISSING).

components.name: cholesterol
components.evidence: GENUINE in milk (animal food) ~10 mg/100g; NOT removed (unlike in plant foods).

components.name: chloride
components.evidence: Major anion; not in USDA panel but well established ~1 g/L.

components.name: riboflavin (vitamin B2)
components.evidence: MAJOR milk vitamin (gives whey its color); was MISSING from current list.

components.name: pantothenic acid (vitamin B5)
components.evidence: Was MISSING; milk is a good B5 source.

components.name: cobalamin (vitamin B12)
components.evidence: Milk is a significant B12 source.

components.name: biotin (vitamin B7)
components.source: Dairy vitamin literature (~1.9-3.4 ug/100g); not in USDA SR panel

components.name: vitamin K
components.evidence: Was MISSING; phylloquinone in milk fat.

components.name: urea
components.source: FooDB; milk urea nitrogen literature (~20-35 mg/100g)
components.evidence: Principal non-protein nitrogen; usable N source.

components.name: L-carnitine
components.evidence: Genuine milk metabolite; acylcarnitines (acetyl-, propionyl-, butyryl-, tetradecanoyl-) present at lower levels.

components.name: taurine
components.source: FooDB; dairy literature (cow milk taurine low, ~1-3 mg/100g)
components.evidence: Free amino sulfonic acid; real but low in bovine milk.

components.name: acetone
components.evidence: Trace ketone body; carried from cow metabolism.

remove.name: Arsenate
remove.reason: non-nutritive trace contaminant heavy-metalloid; not a growth nutrient of milk

remove.name: Cadmium
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant; not a nutrient

remove.name: Boron
remove.reason: ultratrace non-nutritive element; not a defined milk growth nutrient

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: ultratrace contaminant; not a milk growth nutrient

remove.name: D-Galactarate (mucic acid)
remove.reason: D-galactarate/mucic acid is not a normal bovine-milk metabolite; FooDB predicted/implausible

remove.name: D-Ornithine
remove.reason: D-enantiomer implausible; free ornithine is only trace and would be L; likely FooDB artifact

remove.name: D-Serine
remove.reason: mislabeled enantiomer — milk protein serine is L-serine (added correctly above)

remove.name: N-acetyl-L-cysteine
remove.reason: not a natural bovine-milk constituent; likely FooDB predicted compound

remove.name: Methanol
remove.reason: trace volatile artifact, not a growth-relevant nutrient

remove.name: 3-Methylbutanoic acid (isovaleric)
remove.reason: trace flavor volatile, negligible; not growth-relevant

remove.name: L Kynurenine
remove.reason: trace tryptophan catabolite; not a growth nutrient (kept out of core panel)

missing_added: citric acid (0.17 g/100g — principal milk organic acid, was absent)
orotic acid (7 mg/100g — milk-specific pyrimidine acid)
riboflavin/B2 (0.169 mg — major milk vitamin, was absent)
butyric (4:0), myristic (14:0), palmitic (16:0), stearic (18:0), oleic (18:1), linoleic (18:2), alpha-linolenic (18:3) fatty acids
keep_uncovered: phosphatidylcholines PC(16:0/18:1), PC(16:0/18:2), PC(16:0/16:0) etc. (genuine MFGM membrane phospholipids, no BiGG met)
beta-carotene, retinol (carotenoid/retinoid, kept as nutrients above)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 746782 — Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D (SR Legacy)
PMID 1958838 — Variability of orotic acid concentration in cow's milk (mean 0.618 mmol/L)
Agrarforschung Schweiz 2008 — Orotic acid content in cow's, ewe's and goat's milk (6.3-7.6 mg/100g)
PMID 12237966 — Relationship between citrate and ketone bodies in cow's milk
Fox, Uniacke-Lowe, McSweeney & O'Mahony, Dairy Chemistry and Biochemistry (citrate 8-11 mmol/L; free sugars; chloride; myo-inositol; carnitine)
notes: Dominant carbon/energy source is LACTOSE (~4.8 g/100g, ~140 mM); the key growth-relevant organic acid CITRATE (~8.9 mM, ~1.7 g/L) and milk-specific OROTIC acid were absent from the FooDB profile and were added. Fresh pasteurized milk has only trace lactate (rises on souring). Cholesterol is retained (genuine animal-food lipid, unlike the plant-food rule). Removed heavy-metal/contaminant elements (arsenate, cadmium, aluminium, rubidium, boron, nickel) and implausible/mislabeled FooDB entries (D-galactarate, D-ornithine, D-serine, N-acetylcysteine). MFGM phospholipids (PC/SM) are genuine and kept as uncovered.
